title: Loop-the-loop: The people who run around in circles for 24 hours
content:
Loop-the-loop: The people who run around in circles for 24 hours

It is midnight in central London and the rain is bouncing off the ground.

Most of the city is asleep, but on an athletics track just south of the River Thames one man - shivering and soaked to the bone in shorts, T-shirt and makeshift gilet fashioned from a black bin bag - is running laps.

A pensioner, who flew in from Norway that morning, is doing the same in a blue pound-shop poncho.

There is a small pile of vomit on the inside of the track, where a runner emptied his stomach an hour earlier. Job done, he picked himself up and carried on.

Plenty of others have also been sick, including a 74-year-old former librarian. Twice.

It is hardly a surprise. After all, these people have been running around the same track for 12 hours. They have another 12 to go.

Welcome to the world of 24-hour racing, where the boundaries of pain, pleasure and possibility are redefined by a special band of runners who are as exceptional as they are utterly normal.

The format could not be simpler: complete as many laps of the 400m track as you can in 24 hours and whoever clocks up the most miles wins.

But as the puddles swell and the temperature drops in the small hours at Battersea Park Athletics Club, the only thing on runners芒聙聶 minds is survival.

So why do people choose to do this? What keeps them going when their body - and mind - is at breaking point? And how far can a human actually run in 24 hours?

Time stops for no-one - constant movement is the aim in a 24hr race

芒聙聹If there芒聙聶s one thing we芒聙聶ve got in common it芒聙聶s that we芒聙聶re all weird,芒聙聺 says former British record holder Robbie Britton, who has run 12 24hr races.

芒聙聹You芒聙聶re going to have a minimum of 12 hours of pain. There芒聙聶s no other sport where you get to the start line the fittest you芒聙聶ve ever been and, if all goes well, you can芒聙聶t walk properly the next day.芒聙聺

Aleksandr Sorokin is no different. 芒聙聹Absolutely I don芒聙聶t enjoy it. I hate it because I know it芒聙聶s big suffering,芒聙聺 says the man who ran 198 miles to break his own world record in 2022. That is the equivalent of more than seven marathons at 3hr 10min pace, or running a 22min 30sec Parkrun 64 times in a row.

Former GB runner James Elson, a veteran of 13 24hr races, says: 芒聙聹Physically and psychologically it芒聙聶s the most pure running format. The joy and satisfaction of a 24hr race is in its difficulty.芒聙聺

At noon in Battersea, there are nothing but smiles among the 42 runners standing under blue skies on the start line of theSri Chinmoy Self-Transcendence 24hr Track Race.,external

Once the gun goes, the clock doesn芒聙聶t stop. Any time spent stationary - eating, drinking or going to the toilet - is time wasted. Some runners manage all of the above without stopping.

Most charge off as if the race is only one lap, rather than the 527 that the eventual winner completes.

The leaders rattle off a 10k while chatting casually. They have a marathon under their belts inside three and a half hours, a time most recreational runners would be delighted to achieve in a one-off race.

Patricia Seabrook, the oldest competitor at 84, favours a brisk walk. She understands the value of pacing - this is her 19th time at the race, although her personal best of 108 miles from 1996 is no longer in danger.

Why keep coming back? 芒聙聹It芒聙聶s there to be done,芒聙聺 says the former waitress and minibus driver from Somerset, who has also run 522 marathons. 芒聙聹While I can still do it, I will.芒聙聺

Per Audun Heskestad and Patricia Seabrook - combined age 153 - put Sri Chinmoy's words into practice

Ray McCurdy is another playing the long game. A 70-year-old no-nonsense Glaswegian, he has completed 200 marathons and 179 ultra-marathons - anything beyond 26.2 miles - and has been a regular at Sri Chinmoy since 1998. 芒聙聹I芒聙聶m kind of addicted to them,芒聙聺 he shrugs.

John Turner, the ex-librarian from Kent, is chasing his 17th Sri Chinmoy finish. Despite not taking up running until his 30s, he too has more than 200 marathons to his name. 芒聙聹I like a challenge,芒聙聺 he says with a grin that remains on his face for much of the next 24 hours.

Others are experiencing the format for the first time, drawn by the prospect of pushing their body and mind to places they have never been.

芒聙聹So many people are scared of what they don芒聙聶t know. I really embrace that,芒聙聺 says Richard Hall-Smith, a 44-year-old product director from Leicester who started running to lose weight in 2021. 芒聙聹When people ask 芒聙聵why?芒聙聶, I say 芒聙聵why not?芒聙聶芒聙聺

Michael Stocks, who was so moved by his experience of running 155 miles to win the race in 2018 that he wrote a book calledOne Track Mind,external, says: 芒聙聹You芒聙聶re going to learn a lot about yourself and about what芒聙聶s possible. I芒聙聶m seeking to open new doors.芒聙聺

For the vast majority, winning is impossible - and irrelevant. 芒聙聹It芒聙聶s about trying your best for every second of that race,芒聙聺 according to 37-year-old Britton.

Camping tables, car boots and gazebos serve as personal aid stations for the runners

But surely running around in circles is boring? No change in scenery. No variety in terrain. No crowds cheering you along.

芒聙聹For much of my running life, the idea of doing a loop seemed quite ridiculous,芒聙聺 says 55-year-old tech entrepreneur Stocks, who then did 622 of them on the way to victory.

Matt Field, who broke Britton芒聙聶s GB record by running 174 miles in August, says: 芒聙聹I can芒聙聶t say I felt bored throughout the whole 24 hours.芒聙聺

He listens to podcasts (ultra-running ones, naturally) and techno music - 芒聙聹it has to be upbeat, fast and continuous芒聙聺 - while Lithuanian Sorokin prefers Metallica blasting in his ears. Hall-Smith is content with 芒聙聹just my thoughts芒聙聺.

Running repeatedly around the same track has its benefits. You don芒聙聶t have to carry food or drink, you pass your support crew every few minutes and it is impossible to take a wrong turn, unlike most ultra-marathons.

In Fields芒聙聶 words, 24hr racing is 芒聙聹definitely not a spectator sport芒聙聺, which might explain why there is genuine excitement every four hours when all the runners change direction in an attempt to prevent injury.

"I'm not last for once," jokes Seabrook (left) as she makes the turn

Two of the pre-race favourites have dropped out by the five-hour mark thanks to illness and a knee problem, and the leader does not make it beyond 10 hours because of chest pains. 芒聙聹The race doesn芒聙聶t start until 16 hours芒聙聺 is a phrase uttered more than once throughout the day and night by those who have seen it all before.

Yet there are moments of dark humour amid the obvious struggles.

A runner slumps in his chair as the sort of friend we all wish we had mops up his sick from the track with kitchen towel.

At a concert a few yards away in Battersea Park the crowd belt out 芒聙聹I would walk 500 miles芒聙聺. Perhaps the DJ is an ultra-runner as well as a Proclaimers fan.

The hungry - and fearless - local fox sneaks into an open car by the track. He gets away with an energy gel. It could be worse - last year he pinched a runner芒聙聶s spare shoe.

Competitors come armed with a mountain of food. Field, an estimator in the construction industry, consumed 10,000 calories during his record run, made up of carbohydrate-rich gels, chocolate, peanut butter and Turkish Delight. He took 12 Calippos for emergencies but needed only three.

Sorokin, who also holds world records for 100km, 100 miles and 12 hours, enjoys cookies, oranges and sandwiches, alternating between sweet and savoury. 芒聙聹I say to my stomach, 芒聙聵can you eat a banana?芒聙聶 He says 芒聙聵no, no, no - let芒聙聶s try something else芒聙聶.芒聙聺

Velcro stickers and marker pen come in handy as race officials update the leaderboard

Sarah Funderburk, the leading woman as darkness falls at Battersea, is partial to salted potatoes. Brian Robb, the overnight race leader, slurps his way through 57 yoghurt tubes, the sort more commonly seen in a child芒聙聶s lunchbox.

Samantha Hudson dos Santos Figueira (formerly Amend), a GB 24hr runner and the British women芒聙聶s 100-mile record holder, has taken it a step further in the past. 芒聙聹I芒聙聶ve had baby food in a race - because it芒聙聶s easy to get down.芒聙聺

In a 24hr race, eating on the go takes on a very literal meaning. But getting food - or 芒聙聹fuel芒聙聺, as many call it - into your body is not easy, especially after it has taken a pounding for several hours.

芒聙聹I had such a hard time chewing,芒聙聺 Funderburk, an American now living in London, says after the race. 芒聙聹I made loads of jam tortillas but I just didn芒聙聶t want to eat them.芒聙聺

Stocks remembers 芒聙聹gagging even when I looked at food芒聙聺, while Hudson dos Santos Figueira eats raw ginger to combat nausea. 芒聙聹I just munch on it - it芒聙聶s disgusting.芒聙聺

Britton, who coaches some of the world芒聙聶s best ultra-runners, says: 芒聙聹I eat mostly gels. It芒聙聶s painful but you芒聙聶re just getting in as much as you can. They taste OK, but does that matter? I芒聙聶m not eating to enjoy it.芒聙聺

Every long training run is eating practice, according to 37-year-old Field. 芒聙聹I did one where I ate a Pot Noodle and a tin of rice pudding.芒聙聺

He stopped for only 26 minutes of his record-breaking run. Britton was on the go for all but 23 of his.

Although there are portaloos by the side of the track, even those few extra footsteps can seem like an unnecessary diversion. In a 2018 race infamous for its atrocious weather, Stocks remembers shunning social etiquette. 芒聙聹It was late, it was pouring with rain, so why would I bother stopping? I would pee in my pants.芒聙聺

Lined up along the edge of the track are the support crews - normally a partner or friend who has sacrificed their weekend to pull an all-nighter.

Some runners arrive with little more than a plastic bag of snacks and a camping chair. Others operate out of the boot of their car. The best prepared bring a gazebo, fridge and spreadsheet containing a scientific nutrition and hydration strategy.

Minimalism meets aid station

芒聙聹I芒聙聶ve done this a few thousand times,芒聙聺 smiles Rolf Schatzmann as he steps smoothly into the inside lane and hands his wife, Bernadette Benson, a drink he has just prepared. She doesn芒聙聶t need to break stride.

Part of the role of the crew is to offer encouragement and provide motivation - but they must choose their words carefully.

芒聙聹Never ask 芒聙聵how are you doing?芒聙聶 because they芒聙聶re probably in pain,芒聙聺 says Kate Hayden, an ultra-runner who has travelled from Somerset to stand in the rain and help Robb and his partner Roz Glover have the best races they can. She ends up doing the same for three other runners who have no support.

So what should you say? 芒聙聹Ask them 芒聙聵what do you need?芒聙聶芒聙聺 says Hayden - even if that is met more than once with curt replies such as 芒聙聹new legs!芒聙聺 as runners shuffle past.

Shuffling has become the norm as rain lashes down in the early hours of the morning, the runners deep into what is widely regarded as the toughest part of the race.

芒聙聹At night begins the fight between your body, mind and pain. If you win that, you win the race,芒聙聺 says 43-year-old Sorokin, one of the few professional 24hr runners in the world.

Elson describes it as 芒聙聹deep physical and psychological pain芒聙聺 and says the period from eight to 20 hours is 芒聙聹absolutely horrendous芒聙聺.

芒聙聹The biggest challenge is mental. Convincing your brain is absolutely essential,芒聙聺 says Stocks, who works with a sports psychologist. 芒聙聹I芒聙聶m having this constant argument in my head. The voice is telling me 芒聙聵Stop, you don芒聙聶t need to do this. That芒聙聶s sore. This is sore. You芒聙聶ve done enough. This is not that important.芒聙聶 You have to head that voice off at the pass.芒聙聺

Britton says: 芒聙聹When you do a 5k your body is screaming at you to stop. In a 24hr your body is whispering the entire time. It芒聙聶s a constant nag.芒聙聺

John Pares, former Commonwealth 24hr champion and now GB team manager, pulled out of a race once because of blisters. 芒聙聹The guy looking after me took my shoes off and said 芒聙聵there aren芒聙聶t any blisters芒聙聶. I could see blisters on my feet. This is how your unconscious mind comes up with tactics to fool your conscious mind.芒聙聺

Brian Robb takes a rare moment of rest during the overnight storm

Almost a quarter of the field have dropped by 2am, some to injury, some broken by the weather. Benson, a 55-year-old Canadian child psychologist who has travelled from Australia for this race, writes later that she 芒聙聹finally lost the will to live after 13.5 hours芒聙聺.

Despite sitting in second place throughout the night, Funderburk says afterwards: 芒聙聹Many times I wasn芒聙聶t sure I was ever going to finish.芒聙聺

How do runners convince themselves to carry on when, in Elson芒聙聶s words, 芒聙聹there is absolutely no reason to stay out there芒聙聺?

芒聙聹You can芒聙聶t do it for fame or money because there is none,芒聙聺 says Field. 芒聙聹You芒聙聶ve got to have a why.芒聙聺

Glover, a charity worker from Bristol, says it is a privilege to see others achieve their goals - 芒聙聹be it a new distance, a 100-mile personal best or to just battle the demons that tell them to stop芒聙聺.

Hudson dos Santos Figueira picks her favourite motivational quotes from a jar. 芒聙聹I also pinch myself or put Deep Heat on so it burns. In trail races I deliberately run through stinging nettles.芒聙聺

Britton grins his way through the pain. 芒聙聹Smiling impacts your perception of effort - studies have shown it,芒聙聺 he says. 芒聙聹It sends a message through your body that things aren芒聙聶t as hard as they are.

芒聙聹Everyone is hurting. Who can suffer the least? Who can enjoy it? I love those bits. A good 24hr performance isn芒聙聶t made when you芒聙聶re feeling good and moving well. It芒聙聶s made in the tough moments.

芒聙聹If you芒聙聶ve got a very strong mindset, you芒聙聶re going to go further than a very fit person.芒聙聺

The loneliness of the long-distance runner

The rain in London is torrential. The timing clock is broken. A gazebo has blown away. Even the hardy Seabrook has gone for a nap in her car. 芒聙聹She芒聙聶s not normally that sensible,芒聙聺 says her daughter Theresa.

Amid the deluge, the determination of the runners is nothing short of astonishing.

The shivering Robb, a 40-year-old software engineer from Bristol who decided not to bring a jacket despite a weather warning on the forecast, shuffles forward trying to protect his lead. He has finally been persuaded that a bin-bag gilet is better than nothing.

Glover, who is partially sighted and has a congenital heart defect and curvature of the spine, marches on despite blisters all over her feet. She only took up running because she could not drive her daughter, who is deaf, to a special class. Now 51, this is Glover芒聙聶s 20th 24hr race and she has run more than 100 ultra-marathons. She will rack up 89 miles.

McCurdy keeps grinding his way to 46 miles, wrapped in the knee-length standard-issue red coat he wore during his days as a newspaper seller. 芒聙聹I芒聙聶ve had this since 1993,芒聙聺 he says proudly.

Per Audun Heskestad looks as fresh as a 69-year-old in a soggy blue poncho can. He will end the race with four Norwegian records and 108 miles in his legs.

Heskestad refuels in the middle of the night

A strange paradox of 24hr races is that the faster you are, the harder it is - without the pay-off of finishing sooner. And runners芒聙聶 determination to push beyond their limits can pose a different set of problems.

芒聙聹When I ran for GB one of my team-mates was turning yellow because his kidneys were going into malfunction,芒聙聺 says Stocks.

芒聙聹I had blood in my vomit in one race,芒聙聺 says Britton, who nevertheless carried on.

芒聙聹At one race an athlete passed out. Luckily one of our athletes was a paramedic, so he had to jump into paramedic mode.

芒聙聹People can put themselves in very bad places. If you芒聙聶re having a good race it芒聙聶s more likely you芒聙聶ll run yourself into unconsciousness.芒聙聺

Adversity visibly brings the runners together in Battersea, whether it be a fist bump while overtaking, or pausing mid-track to celebrate others reaching 100 miles. In endearingly low-key fashion, the landmark moment takes place besides a bin.

Aside from the usual blisters, limps and throbbing limbs, most runners escape relatively unscathed. Simon Bennett, a 65-year-old semi-retired writer from Pontefract, shrugs off a suspected case of trench foot as if he had stubbed his toe.

The weather wreaks havoc on runners' feet and bodies

芒聙聹I love the shared suffering,芒聙聺 says 42-year-old Elson, who runs a company that organises ultra-marathons. Stocks says: 芒聙聹We芒聙聶re a community doing this crazy thing. It芒聙聶s this little ecosystem of life.芒聙聺

Even the volunteers and crew are a special breed. One has swum the English Channel seven times. Another is a former British cycling hill-climb champion. Race referee Hilary Walker once held the 24hr and 48hr world records and is a member of the Ultra-running Hall of Fame. Pam Storey, who is 76 and crewing two runners, has more than 200 marathons and 24 24hr races on her CV.

One of only three 24hr track events each year in the UK - along with Crawley, organised by Storey, and Gloucester - the race was founded in 1989 by the late spiritual guri Sri Chinmoy, who promoted meditation and physical activity.

Despite the Self-Transcendence tagline, few runners will reach an elevated state within 24 hours - that芒聙聶s where a3,100-mile racecomes in handy - but the event serves as mindfulness for some.

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s like therapy,芒聙聺 says 45-year-old Hudson dos Santos Figueira, who works in IT. 芒聙聹I芒聙聶ve had a lot of bad experiences - my husband passed away and I芒聙聶ve had a lot of deaths close to me. I get that sense of comfort when I run.芒聙聺

Stocks says: 芒聙聹The dream is to be in the zone as much as possible. You have periods when you will lose portions of time.芒聙聺

The race does not make a profit - the number of runners is capped because of limited space on the track - but race director Shankara Smith says she will 芒聙聹keep doing it for as long as the runners want to do it芒聙聺.

芒聙聹I find it inspirational to see what people can do,芒聙聺 she says. 芒聙聹When you reset your expectations it芒聙聶s amazing.芒聙聺

Ray McCurdle, wearing his 31-year-old jacket and a race belt made from a bin bag, keeps on smiling

Spirits on the track soar as the sun rises and the rain stops.

芒聙聹That was the best place in the world - it was magical,芒聙聺 says Hall-Smith, who had to spend half an hour in a hot shower at 4am to stave off hypothermia.

Spurred on by the news that she is only one lap behind Robb, Funderburk ups her pace. Her partner and support crew Sean Collum is not surprised. 芒聙聹She芒聙聶s so competitive,芒聙聺 he says. 芒聙聹We played board games on our first date - she absolutely hammered me.芒聙聺

By 7am Funderburk has taken the lead and will never relinquish it. Cheered on by members of her running club, the 42-year-old who works in medical communications will become only the third woman in history to win the race. In her first 24hr event, she covers 131 miles, enough to qualify for the US team. By a curious quirk of fate, it marks 10 years since her first half-marathon - a mere 13.1 miles.

The final few hours bring a joyous atmosphere. Friends arrive bearing good wishes and, in Funderburk芒聙聶s case, hash browns. Smiles are back and layers are off. Robb芒聙聶s bin-bag gilet is no more.

As the race enters the last hour, those who resembled zombies not so long ago are now running like Mo Farah. Some even manage a sprint in the dying seconds, desperate to bag as many miles as they can.

When the end does come, after 24 of the toughest hours of their athletic lives, there is no glorious finish line or roaring grandstand. Instead, runners must stop wherever they are and place a small pot of sand by the track.

Although electronic chip timers record runners' completed laps, the unfinished final loop is measured - to three decimal places - by a race adjudicator with a wheel straight from a 1990s PE lesson.

Every centimetre counts

Funderburk芒聙聶s first thoughts when the race ends? 芒聙聹Absolute, utter relief.芒聙聺

For Hall-Smith, it is pride. 芒聙聹It芒聙聶s like you can step away and shake your own hand and say well done,芒聙聺 he says. 芒聙聹We don芒聙聶t do that enough in life.芒聙聺

Almost all the 29 runners who have survived the race collapse to the ground. Others have to be helped into chairs. At the modest presentation ceremony, most have their small trophy brought to them because they can barely stand to collect it.

芒聙聹Sometimes in training you think what you will do when you finish - raise your hands, smile, punch the air,芒聙聺 says world record holder Sorokin. 芒聙聹In reality, you just turn off like a robot.芒聙聺

Funderburk says a day later: 芒聙聹My feet and ankles are a mess. I芒聙聶m still not sure when I芒聙聶ll be able to walk.芒聙聺

Stocks had to crawl up the stairs at home, needed help getting dressed and was slurring his words for days after his best 24hr race.

So why - just why - would any normal person put themselves through all this?

芒聙聹Imagine being described as normal,芒聙聺 says Britton. 芒聙聹That would be rubbish, wouldn芒聙聶t it?芒聙聺

Sarah Funberburk, wrapped in a survival blanket, takes the applause during the presentation ceremony

'I gave everything to boxing and still have nothing'

'It was like a non-league team asking Man Utd for a match'

'The cash came up to my torso' -  tales of a match-fixer
